items.content:
RT @OSdev_: CVE-2022-22047 is a Windows local privilege escalation vulnerability in CSRSS that introduced researchers to a new bug class: Activation Context Cache Poisoning.

By poisoning CSRSS's activation context cache, an attacker can influence how privileged processes resolve and load DLL dependencies, ultimately leading to SYSTEM privileges. It's an excellent case study in CSRSS, activation contexts, SxS manifests, and Windows DLL loading internals.

https://t.co/sn3pycutfN

items.content:
Orange Tsai found hidden character transformations buried in Windows ANSI encoding that most applications have no idea exist.

WorstFit exploits how Windows silently converts characters during encoding, creating attack vectors across countless applications that trust their input is what they think it is.

From the same researcher who brought us ProxyLogon, ProxyShell, ProxyOracle, ProxyRelay, and hacked Facebook twice. Studying his older work on Exchange attack surfaces is still one of the best ways to learn how to think about vulnerability research.

https://t.co/afEXnodLb7

Author: @orange_8361

#InfoSec #BugBounty #WebSecurity

items.author: 0xocdsec

items.content:
RT @askalphaxiv: DeepSeek just published DSpark, a speculative decoding system that boosts live DeepSeek V4 serving throughput by 51% to 406% under stricter latency targets.

With how most speculative decoding methods draft more tokens, but waste verification compute when those tokens get rejected, DSpark fixes this with a semi autoregressive drafter for more coherent long drafts, plus a confidence scheduler that only verifies prefixes likely to survive.

It also gives 60% to 85% faster per user generation at matched throughput.

items.author: 0xocdsec

items.content:
RT @OSdev_: Apple's Neural Engine (ANE) is far more than an "AI accelerator." A recent reverse-engineering effort reveals a tightly integrated dataflow processor optimized for neural network inference.

• Uses software-managed scratchpad SRAM instead of CPU-style caches.
• Streams tensors via DMA into matrix compute engines, minimizing DRAM traffic.
• CoreML compiles and fuses operators (Conv + BatchNorm + ReLU) into optimized command buffers before execution.
• Firmware schedules command queues, DMA transfers, synchronization, and power management, applications never access the hardware directly.
• Performance depends on arithmetic intensity: low AI workloads are memory-bandwidth bound, while high-intensity workloads approach peak TOPS.
• Compressed weights and low-precision formats (FP16/INT8) reduce bandwidth and improve throughput.

The key takeaway: Apple's advantage isn't just the ANE hardware, it's the vertically integrated stack spanning CoreML, compiler, runtime, kernel driver, firmware, and accelerator, all co-designed to maximize inference performance per watt.

items.author: 0xocdsec

items.content:
RT @nextronresearch: WHQL-signed kernel driver with a full network-accessible backdoor

wskmon.sys registers a WFP stream callout, intercepts inbound TCP traffic, searches for a custom NTF packet, verifies HMAC-SHA256, decrypts the payload, and executes commands from kernel context.

- No IOCTL.
- No user-mode agent.
- No injected DLL.

Any inbound TCP service can act as transport. In our demo, a simple Python HTTP server receives a POST request while the driver already processed the payload below it.

Nice reminder that “WHQL-signed driver” does not mean “safe driver”

Full analysis
https://t.co/c18rkWhGuw

items.author: cr3ghost

items.content:
Phrack 72 published the analysis of an actual North Korean APT workstation dump.

Real Kimsuky source code. A kernel-level remote backdoor. A private Cobalt Strike beacon. Android malware. Stolen South Korean government GPKI certificates. Access to the Defense Counterintelligence Command and Ministry of Foreign Affairs.

A South Korean security firm independently confirmed the findings. They matched the leaked rootkit source code to a rootkit found during a real 2022 incident at a South Korean financial institution. Same code, same encryption keys.

Opens with “Dear Kimsuky, you are no hacker.”

https://t.co/kaMJUzmaLr 

https://t.co/YJ7glibYHQ

Authors: Saber + cyb0rg

Published in @phrack Issue 72, 40th Anniversary

Follow-up analysis by ENKI White

#ThreatIntel #Malware #InfoSec
